name: Azure DevOps Services Overview and Organization Setup
description: Configuring Azure DevOps organizations, projects, teams, and integrating with GitHub for source control and project tracking.
key_facts: Azure DevOps includes: Boards (work tracking), Repos (Git/TFVC), Pipelines (CI/CD), Test Plans, and Artifacts
Organizations are the top-level container; projects sit below organizations and contain all Azure DevOps services
Area paths and iteration paths organize work items for team assignment and sprint planning
Azure DevOps supports GitHub as an external repository source for pipelines without migrating to Azure Repos
Service connections securely store credentials for external services (Azure subscription, Docker Hub, npm, etc.)
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Azure DevOps and GitHub are separate products with no integration—Microsoft fully integrates both; GitHub Actions can trigger Azure Pipelines and vice versa
Many believe all Azure DevOps features require the full Azure DevOps stack—individual services like Boards can be used standalone with GitHub Repos

name: Agile Work Management with Azure Boards
description: Implementing Agile, Scrum, and Kanban processes using Azure Boards for sprint planning, backlog management, and team dashboards.
key_facts: Azure Boards work item types: Epic > Feature > User Story > Task (Agile); Epic > Feature > PBI > Task (Scrum)
Kanban boards show work in progress with WIP limits to enforce flow and reduce multitasking
Sprint burndown and velocity charts measure team progress and predictability across sprints
Delivery Plans provide a cross-team, cross-project roadmap view of work items over time
GitHub issues and pull requests can be linked to Azure Boards work items for end-to-end traceability
common_misconceptions: Teams assume switching process templates (Agile to Scrum) is easy—process template migration requires careful planning as work item types differ
Many believe Azure Boards velocity charts automatically improve sprint accuracy—velocity only measures the past; estimation quality depends on team calibration

name: Git Branching Strategies and Source Control Design
description: Designing branching strategies (trunk-based, Gitflow, GitHub Flow) and pull request policies for effective team collaboration.
key_facts: Trunk-based development uses short-lived feature branches merged frequently to main; enables continuous integration
Gitflow uses long-lived develop, release, and hotfix branches; better for scheduled releases with multiple versions
Branch policies in Azure Repos enforce code review requirements, build validation, and linked work items
Git hooks (pre-commit, pre-push) run scripts locally; branch policies run on the server enforcing standards
Large File Storage (Git LFS) tracks large binary files separately to keep repository size manageable
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Gitflow is the universal best practice—trunk-based development is now preferred for CI/CD as long-lived branches cause integration problems
Many believe branch policies prevent direct pushes automatically—branch policies must be explicitly configured; they do not apply by default on new branches

name: Pull Request Management and Code Review
description: Implementing effective pull request workflows with required reviewers, build validation, and automated code quality checks.
key_facts: Required reviewers can be set at branch policy level; auto-completion waits for approvals before merging
Build validation runs a CI pipeline as part of the PR check; failures block merge
Status checks from external services (SonarQube, GitHub Actions) integrate as PR gates
Squash merge condenses all PR commits to one; rebase merge preserves history without merge commits
Draft pull requests signal work-in-progress and prevent accidental merge while still enabling early feedback
common_misconceptions: Teams assume requiring many approvers improves quality linearly—too many required approvers slows delivery; 1-2 reviewers with automated checks is typically optimal
Many believe squash merge is always preferable—squash loses individual commit context which can be valuable for debugging history

name: Azure Pipelines CI Build Configuration
description: Designing and implementing continuous integration pipelines using YAML-based Azure Pipelines with agents, triggers, and multi-stage builds.
key_facts: YAML pipelines are version-controlled alongside code; classic UI pipelines are being phased out
Pipeline agents: Microsoft-hosted (fresh environment each run) and self-hosted (persistent, faster for large builds)
Trigger types: CI (push), PR, scheduled, and pipeline completion triggers for multi-pipeline dependencies
Pipeline caching (cache task) speeds up builds by restoring dependencies between runs using a cache key
Multi-stage YAML pipelines define build and deployment stages in a single file with stage dependencies
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Microsoft-hosted agents are always slower—for most builds they are fast enough; self-hosted makes sense only for large artifact downloads or custom tool requirements
Many believe YAML pipelines require all stages in one file—templates allow splitting stages across multiple files and reusing common pipeline components

name: Continuous Delivery and Deployment Strategies
description: Implementing deployment strategies including blue-green, canary, rolling, and ring-based deployments with approval gates.
key_facts: Blue-green deployment maintains two identical environments; traffic switches instantly reducing rollback time to seconds
Canary releases gradually shift traffic percentage to the new version while monitoring error rates
Rolling deployment updates instances in batches; half-in-half-out reduces blast radius during deployment
Deployment gates (pre/post) run automated checks (Azure Monitor alerts, REST API) before proceeding to next stage
Feature flags decouple deployment from release; code ships dark and features are toggled on progressively
common_misconceptions: Teams assume blue-green eliminates all downtime—session state and database schema changes can still cause issues during traffic switch without careful planning
Many believe canary releases require special Azure infrastructure—Azure App Service deployment slots and Azure Front Door weighted routing enable canary natively

name: GitHub Actions for CI/CD
description: Building CI/CD workflows using GitHub Actions, including reusable workflows, composite actions, and integration with Azure services.
key_facts: GitHub Actions workflows are YAML files in .github/workflows/ triggered by events (push, PR, schedule, workflow_dispatch)
Jobs run on runners (GitHub-hosted or self-hosted) and consist of steps (Actions or shell commands)
Reusable workflows (workflow_call trigger) allow sharing workflow logic across repositories
OIDC federated identity enables passwordless authentication to Azure without storing secrets in GitHub
GitHub Environments provide deployment protection rules including required reviewers and wait timers
common_misconceptions: Teams assume GitHub Actions and Azure Pipelines have equivalent feature sets—Azure Pipelines has more mature deployment gates; GitHub Actions has better open-source community integration
Many believe secrets stored in GitHub are encrypted end-to-end—secrets are encrypted at rest in GitHub but are exposed as environment variables in runners during job execution

name: Infrastructure as Code with Bicep and Terraform
description: Implementing Infrastructure as Code using Azure Bicep and HashiCorp Terraform for repeatable, auditable infrastructure deployments.
key_facts: Azure Bicep is a DSL that transpiles to ARM JSON; it is the recommended IaC language for Azure-native deployments
Terraform uses HCL and maintains state files; remote state in Azure Storage enables team collaboration
Bicep modules decompose infrastructure into reusable components analogous to ARM linked templates
Terraform plan shows a dry-run of changes before apply; equivalent to Bicep what-if deployment mode
ARM deployment modes: Complete (removes unlisted resources) vs Incremental (default, only adds/updates resources)
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Terraform is always better than Bicep for Azure—Bicep has first-party support, day-0 new Azure resource support, and simpler state management for Azure-only workloads
Many believe IaC eliminates all manual configuration drift—IaC manages what it knows about; resources created outside IaC cause drift that must be imported or managed separately

name: Azure Artifacts and Package Management
description: Managing package feeds in Azure Artifacts for NuGet, npm, Maven, Python, and Universal Packages with upstream sources.
key_facts: Azure Artifacts hosts private feeds for NuGet, npm, Maven, Python (pip), and Universal Packages
Upstream sources allow feeds to proxy public registries (NuGet.org, npmjs.com) saving bandwidth and ensuring availability
Feed views (Release, Prerelease, Local) control which package versions are promoted and visible to consumers
Universal Packages store arbitrary binary artifacts (ML models, executables) in Azure Artifacts with versioning
Symbol packages (.snupkg) in Azure Artifacts enable source debugging by linking PDB symbols to source code
common_misconceptions: Teams assume upstream sources pull all packages eagerly—upstream sources only cache packages the first time they are requested, not proactively
Many believe Azure Artifacts is only for internal packages—upstream sources make it a proxy cache for all public packages improving reliability and security scanning

name: DevSecOps and Security Scanning in Pipelines
description: Integrating security scanning tools including SAST, DAST, dependency scanning, and container scanning into CI/CD pipelines.
key_facts: SAST (Static Application Security Testing) scans source code for vulnerabilities without execution; tools: SonarQube, Checkmarx
Dependency scanning checks third-party libraries for known CVEs; GitHub Dependabot and Mend (WhiteSource) automate this
DAST (Dynamic Application Security Testing) tests running applications for vulnerabilities; tools: OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite
Container image scanning (Trivy, Aqua, Defender for Containers) checks for OS and app layer vulnerabilities
Microsoft Defender for DevOps integrates security posture management directly into GitHub and Azure DevOps
common_misconceptions: Teams assume SAST alone is sufficient for application security—SAST misses runtime-only vulnerabilities; DAST and penetration testing complement it
Many believe scanning containers at build time is enough—containers should be re-scanned periodically after deployment as new CVEs are discovered daily

name: Secret Management with Azure Key Vault in Pipelines
description: Integrating Azure Key Vault with Azure Pipelines and GitHub Actions to securely retrieve secrets, certificates, and keys at runtime.
key_facts: Azure Key Vault variable groups in Azure Pipelines dynamically retrieve secrets at pipeline runtime
Key Vault references in App Service and Azure Functions automatically rotate secrets without redeployment
RBAC model for Key Vault uses Key Vault Administrator, Key Vault Secrets User, and Key Vault Reader roles
Soft delete and purge protection prevent accidental or malicious secret deletion with a retention period
Key Vault access policies (legacy) and Azure RBAC (recommended) are two separate authorization models that can coexist but RBAC is preferred
common_misconceptions: Teams assume pipeline environment variables and Key Vault secrets are equally secure—pipeline variables are encrypted at rest but visible in logs if not properly masked; Key Vault secrets never appear in logs
Many believe adding Key Vault access policies grants RBAC permissions—access policies and RBAC are independent; setting one does not set the other

name: Application Insights and Distributed Tracing
description: Implementing application monitoring with Application Insights including custom telemetry, distributed traces, and live metrics for DevOps feedback loops.
key_facts: Application Insights SDK instruments apps to send traces, requests, dependencies, exceptions, and custom events
Distributed tracing links telemetry across microservices using correlation IDs (operation_Id) for end-to-end visibility
Availability tests (URL ping and multi-step web tests) run from global Azure locations to detect outages
Smart Detection automatically analyzes telemetry patterns and alerts on anomalies without manual threshold configuration
Sampling (adaptive and fixed-rate) reduces telemetry volume and cost while preserving statistical representation
common_misconceptions: Teams assume instrumentation with Application Insights SDK is always required—Azure Monitor agent and codeless attach (for .NET and Java) enable monitoring without SDK code changes
Many believe all Application Insights data is retained indefinitely—default retention is 90 days; logs sent to a Log Analytics workspace can have longer retention configured

name: Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) Strategy
description: Implementing SRE practices including SLI, SLO, SLA definitions, error budgets, chaos engineering, and postmortem processes.
key_facts: SLI (Service Level Indicator) is a quantitative measure of service behavior (e.g., request success rate)
SLO (Service Level Objective) is the target value or range for an SLI (e.g., 99.9% success rate over 30 days)
Error budget is the allowable unreliability (1 - SLO); when exhausted, new deployments are frozen
Chaos engineering deliberately injects faults to verify resilience; Azure Chaos Studio is the native tool
Blameless postmortems focus on system failure analysis rather than individual blame to build psychological safety
common_misconceptions: Teams assume SLA and SLO are the same—SLA is a contractual commitment to customers with penalties; SLO is an internal target that should be stricter than the SLA
Many believe chaos engineering should only be done in non-production environments—mature organizations run controlled chaos experiments in production to find real-world failure modes

name: Container CI/CD with AKS and Azure Container Registry
description: Building containerized application CI/CD pipelines using Azure Container Registry, AKS, and Helm charts for deployment management.
key_facts: Azure Container Registry (ACR) stores and manages private container images with geo-replication for global deployments
ACR Tasks automate image builds triggered by source code commits or base image updates
Helm charts package Kubernetes manifests into versioned, reusable deployment units with parameterizable values
AKS deployments can use rolling update strategy with maxSurge and maxUnavailable controls for zero-downtime
GitOps with Flux or Argo CD synchronizes the desired state from a Git repository to the AKS cluster continuously
common_misconceptions: Teams assume Docker Hub is adequate for production container storage—Docker Hub has rate limits and lacks enterprise features like geo-replication, RBAC, and vulnerability scanning that ACR provides
Many believe Helm replaces Kubernetes manifests—Helm is a package manager that generates Kubernetes manifests; the underlying manifests still need to be well-designed

name: Automated Testing Strategy in DevOps Pipelines
description: Implementing a comprehensive testing strategy including unit, integration, regression, and load tests within CI/CD pipelines using Azure Test Plans.
key_facts: Testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, cheap), fewer integration tests, fewer UI/E2E tests (slow, expensive)
Azure Test Plans manages manual test cases, exploratory testing, and tracks test results across releases
Azure Load Testing uses Apache JMeter scripts to simulate thousands of concurrent users and identify bottlenecks
Test results published in pipelines (PublishTestResults task) are tracked over time in Azure DevOps Test analytics
Shift-left testing moves testing earlier in the pipeline to catch defects before they reach production
common_misconceptions: Teams assume 100% code coverage guarantees quality—coverage measures lines executed, not the quality of assertions; tests can execute code without verifying behavior
Many believe Azure Load Testing requires a real production dataset—load tests should use representative (sanitized) data; running against production data risks data integrity issues
